_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and management per Collins PW et al. Blood 2007 (PMID 17047148), EACH2 registry Knoebl et al. J Thromb Haemost 2012 (PMID 22212058), and ASH 2025 guidelines on acquired hemophilia. Emicizumab data from Knoebl et al. Blood 2021 (PMID 33693659). UKHCDO/EACH2 registry data for outcomes.

h1Title: Acquired Hemophilia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acquired hemophilia is a rare but life-threatening autoimmune disorder in which the immune system produces antibodies (inhibitors) that neutralize clotting Factor VIII — or less commonly Factor V, IX, XI, or XIII — causing spontaneous severe bleeding in patients with no prior bleeding history. The annual incidence is approximately 1.3–1.5 per million per year, predominantly affecting adults over 65 and postpartum women. Unlike inherited hemophilia, acquired hemophilia presents as sudden-onset deep muscle hematomas, large subcutaneous bleeds, and mucosal hemorrhage without a family history. Diagnosis depends on detecting an isolated prolonged APTT with low Factor VIII activity and a positive Factor VIII inhibitor assay. Treatment has two simultaneous goals: stop the acute bleeding with bypassing agents (recombinant Factor VIIa or FEIBA) and eradicate the inhibitor with immunosuppression.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1.3–1.5 per million per year in Europe (EACH2 registry, 2012); incidence increases sharply with age, reaching 14–15 per million per year over age 85
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal distribution: elderly patients over 65 (most common, often with no identifiable trigger) and postpartum women aged 20–40; slight male predominance in elderly cohorts
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis approximately 74 years in population-based studies; postpartum cases typically age 25–35
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1,000–1,500 new cases annually in Europe; similar rates estimated in North America; significant under-diagnosis in settings where APTT mixing studies and Factor VIII assays are not routine

definition: Acquired hemophilia (ICD-10: D68.311) is an acquired coagulopathy caused by autoantibodies directed against coagulation Factor VIII (FVIII), resulting in rapid neutralization or accelerated clearance of FVIII from the circulation. In contrast to congenital hemophilia A (X-linked, affecting males from birth), acquired hemophilia occurs in adults of either sex with no prior bleeding history and no family history of bleeding disorders. The inhibitory autoantibodies belong predominantly to the IgG4 subclass and act by blocking the C2 and A2 domains of FVIII, impairing its interactions with phospholipid surfaces and other clotting factors. The clinical hallmark is a pattern of bleeding distinct from inherited hemophilia: large, painful soft-tissue and retroperitoneal hematomas and mucosal hemorrhage (GI, urinary, respiratory), rather than the hemarthroses typical of congenital disease. The condition is managed by specialist hematologists, typically in tertiary hemostasis centers, requiring a dual approach of hemostatic control and immune suppression. Rarely, inhibitors target other clotting factors (FV, FIX, FXIII, FXI, vWF), producing distinct clinical coagulopathy syndromes.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, accounting for over 95% of acquired hemophilia cases. Autoantibodies neutralize coagulation Factor VIII. Bleeding is typically severe with deep muscle hematomas, retroperitoneal bleeds, and mucosal hemorrhage. Treated with bypassing agents and immunosuppression.
No underlying condition identified in approximately 50% of cases, predominantly elderly patients. Inhibitor frequently remits with immunosuppression in 70–90% of patients but relapses in 15–20%.

typesClassification.type: Associated acquired hemophilia (secondary to underlying disease or trigger)
typesClassification.description: Approximately 50% of cases are associated with an identifiable condition: autoimmune diseases (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis), pregnancy, malignancy (solid tumors, lymphoma, myeloma), medications (penicillin, clopidogrel, interferons), or infections. Resolution of the underlying trigger can facilitate inhibitor eradication.

typesClassification.type: Acquired inhibitors to other clotting factors (Factor V, IX, XI, XIII, vWF)
typesClassification.description: Rare disorders: anti-FV inhibitors can follow exposure to topical bovine thrombin (in surgical sealants); acquired von Willebrand disease is associated with myeloproliferative neoplasms and monoclonal gammopathy; acquired FXI deficiency occurs with SLE and antiphospholipid antibodies.

primarySymptoms: Large, painful spontaneous subcutaneous and deep muscle hematomas — the characteristic presentation in over 80% of cases, most commonly in the thighs, calves, forearms, and retroperitoneum.
Massive bruising (ecchymoses) out of all proportion to any trauma — large purple-blue discolorations over the trunk, extremities, or flanks, often spreading widely.
Prolonged or excessive bleeding after minor procedures or surgery — dental extractions, biopsy, venipuncture, or catheter insertion causing disproportionate hemorrhage.
Gross hematuria without urinary tract pathology — presenting in up to 20% of cases; painful and distressing but rarely causing serious renal obstruction.
GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia) without an identifiable mucosal lesion on endoscopy, from submucosal hematomas.
Retroperitoneal hematoma causing flank or groin pain, hip flexion contracture, and femoral nerve palsy when the iliopsoas muscle is involved.
Hemoptysis or pulmonary hemorrhage — uncommon but potentially fatal presentation requiring urgent hemostatic therapy.
Post-partum hemorrhage that fails to respond to standard uterotonic therapy, occurring days to weeks after delivery in postpartum acquired hemophilia.
Compartment syndrome from muscle hematoma expanding within fascial compartments, causing severe limb pain, loss of pulse, and threatened limb viability.
Absence of joint bleeding (hemarthroses) — a key distinguishing feature from congenital hemophilia A, where joint bleeds are the most common manifestation.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained isolated prolonged APTT with a normal PT on routine coagulation screen — the single most important laboratory trigger for acquired hemophilia workup.
Spontaneous large ecchymoses or hematomas in an elderly patient or postpartum woman with no anticoagulant use and no prior bleeding history.
Excessive bleeding after a minor procedure (dental extraction, skin biopsy) in a patient with normal platelet count and no anticoagulant.
A previously normal APTT that has become prolonged on follow-up testing in a patient being evaluated for an autoimmune condition or malignancy.
emergencySigns: Retroperitoneal hemorrhage with flank pain, hypotension, and expanding hematoma on CT — demands immediate bypassing agent therapy and urgent hematology consult.
Airway compromise or neck swelling from cervical or pharyngeal hematoma — requires airway protection as the highest priority alongside hemostatic therapy.

Acute compartment syndrome: severe escalating limb pain out of proportion to examination, tense swollen muscle compartment, and diminishing pulses — requires fasciotomy alongside hemostatic treatment.
Life-threatening GI or pulmonary hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability — immediate bypassing agent infusion and ICU-level supportive care.
New onset of profound weakness or severe abdominal pain in a patient with a known prolonged APTT — suspect retroperitoneal or psoas hematoma; urgent CT abdomen and hemostatic treatment.

causes.description: The largest subgroup, accounting for 45–50% of cases, predominantly in adults over 70. The reason elderly individuals are predisposed is unknown but is hypothesized to involve immune senescence, accumulation of low-level anti-FVIII B cell clones, and reduced tolerance mechanisms. No underlying disease is identified despite comprehensive workup.

causes.cause: Autoimmune disorders (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease)
causes.description: Approximately 10–15% of acquired hemophilia cases are associated with systemic autoimmune diseases. Dysregulated B cell activity in these conditions facilitates anti-FVIII antibody production. Treating the underlying autoimmune disease alongside immunosuppression for the inhibitor improves outcomes.

Postpartum acquired hemophilia accounts for approximately 8–10% of cases, usually presenting 1–4 months after delivery with unexpected postpartum hemorrhage or subcutaneous bleeding. Inhibitor titers often decrease spontaneously after pregnancy, but treatment is usually still required to control acute bleeding and accelerate eradication.

causes.cause: Malignancy (solid tumors, lymphoma, plasma cell dyscrasia)
causes.description: Approximately 10–15% of cases are associated with malignancy. The mechanism is thought to involve tumor-driven B cell dysregulation and altered antigen presentation. The inhibitor may develop before, during, or after cancer diagnosis. Treating the underlying malignancy may contribute to inhibitor eradication, though immunosuppression is still required.

causes.cause: Drug exposures (penicillin, ampicillin, clopidogrel, interferon-alpha, checkpoint inhibitors)
causes.description: Drug-induced acquired hemophilia is reported with numerous agents, most commonly penicillin antibiotics and, increasingly, immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) in the context of cancer immunotherapy. Inhibitor typically resolves after drug withdrawal, but bypassing agents and corticosteroids are required during the acute phase.

Acquired hemophilia has been described following COVID-19, hepatitis C, HIV, and rarely Epstein-Barr virus infection. Infection-triggered cases may resolve with immunosuppression and infection treatment, but acute hemostatic management is required regardless of trigger.

riskFactors.description: Incidence rises exponentially with age, reaching 14–15 per million per year over age 85 (EACH2 registry 2012). Age-related immune dysregulation is thought to permit emergence of anti-FVIII autoantibody-producing B cell clones.

Postpartum women have a distinct risk window — up to 1 year after delivery. FVIII levels rise during pregnancy, and postpartum immune reactivation may trigger autoantibody production against the suddenly falling FVIII level.

riskFactors.factor: Underlying autoimmune disease (SLE, RA, IBD, Sjögren syndrome)
riskFactors.description: Pre-existing systemic autoimmunity is the most common identifiable predisposing condition in non-elderly patients. These conditions share dysregulated B cell tolerance mechanisms that permit anti-FVIII autoantibody production.

The EACH2 registry identified malignancy in approximately 12% of acquired hemophilia patients; lymphoproliferative disorders are most strongly associated. Paraneoplastic immune stimulation is the presumed mechanism.

Pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab, and other checkpoint inhibitors cause immune-related adverse events including acquired hemophilia. Incidence is estimated at 0.01–0.05% of treated patients; awareness allows early detection and management.
Beta-lactam antibiotics are among the most commonly reported drug triggers; the mechanism is unclear but may involve hapten formation or molecular mimicry with FVIII epitopes.

riskFactors.factor: History of previous inhibitor development after blood product exposure
riskFactors.description: While most acquired hemophilia is de novo, prior exposure to FVIII-containing concentrates in the context of congenital hemophilia or major surgery creates immune memory that can accelerate inhibitor titers in subsequent exposures.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 65 are the largest affected group; incidence increases steeply with age and the majority of cases in registry series are diagnosed after age 70.
Postpartum women aged 20–40 constitute a distinct second peak; postpartum acquired hemophilia has a favorable prognosis compared with elderly cases.
Male-to-female ratio is approximately 1.4:1 in most series, with elderly men slightly more affected; among postpartum cases, all affected patients are women by definition.
No strong ethnic predominance has been established; most registry data comes from European centers (UK, France, Germany, Italy), with smaller US and Asian registry data largely consistent.
Malignancy-associated cases cluster in older adults, reflecting the age distribution of both acquired hemophilia and solid-tumor cancers.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis begins with recognizing the characteristic laboratory pattern: an isolated prolonged APTT (typically 60–120 seconds, with a normal upper limit of 35–40 seconds) with a normal prothrombin time (PT) and normal platelet count. This combination narrows the differential to Factor VIII, IX, XI, or XII deficiency, or a lupus anticoagulant. The next step is an APTT mixing study: mixing equal volumes of patient plasma and normal pooled plasma corrects APTT in deficiency states but fails to correct in the presence of an inhibitor, demonstrating the inhibitory nature of the abnormality. Incubation of the mixed sample at 37°C for 1–2 hours may be needed to reveal time-dependent (Type II kinetics) FVIII inhibitors.

Factor VIII activity assay confirms severe FVIII deficiency (typically below 1 IU/dL in active inhibitor) and quantifies the inhibitor titer using the Bethesda assay or the modified Nijmegen Bethesda assay. An inhibitor titer above 5 Bethesda Units (BU/mL) is considered high titer; below 5 BU/mL is low titer. Both groups require bypassing agent therapy because even low-titer inhibitors render FVIII replacement ineffective in most situations. A comprehensive search for an underlying cause is mandatory: CBC, ESR, CRP, autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-dsDNA, RF), hepatitis B and C serology, HIV, and CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis to exclude occult malignancy in adults over 50.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for coagulation factor deficiency; isolated prolonged APTT without elevated PT is the key laboratory finding in acquired hemophilia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood draw in a citrate tube; result within 1 hour. APTT above 50–60 seconds with normal PT and platelets in a patient with unexpected bleeding should trigger immediate specialist referral.

diagnosticTests.test: APTT mixing study (patient plasma + normal pooled plasma, immediate and 1-hour incubation)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes inhibitor from factor deficiency; no correction of APTT after mixing indicates an inhibitor rather than a deficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two venous blood tubes. The lab mixes samples and measures APTT at time 0 and after 1–2 hours at 37°C. Results in 2–4 hours. Failure to correct after mixing and especially after incubation is the hallmark of Factor VIII inhibitor.

diagnosticTests.test: Factor VIII activity assay (one-stage APTT-based clotting assay)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies residual FVIII clotting activity; confirms severe deficiency (often < 1 IU/dL) in acquired hemophilia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; sent to a coagulation reference laboratory. Result in 4–8 hours in urgent cases. Assay distinguishes acquired hemophilia from other inhibitor-driven prolonged APTT (lupus anticoagulant, which does not reduce factor activity to < 1 IU/dL).

diagnosticTests.test: Factor VIII inhibitor titer (Bethesda or Nijmegen Bethesda assay)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the inhibitor in Bethesda Units (BU/mL); guides hemostatic therapy selection and monitors response to immunosuppression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialized coagulation laboratory test; results in 24–48 hours. Higher titers (> 5 BU/mL) indicate that standard FVIII replacement will be rapidly neutralized and bypassing agents are needed.

diagnosticTests.test: CT imaging of chest, abdomen, and pelvis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies occult malignancy (solid tumor, lymphoma) as an underlying trigger in adults over 50, and detects retroperitoneal hematomas
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15–20 minute scan with IV contrast. Performed in all adults with newly diagnosed acquired hemophilia to guide management of both the inhibitor and any underlying malignancy.

diagnosticTests.test: Autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-dsDNA, RF, ANCA, antiphospholipid antibodies)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies autoimmune conditions (SLE, RA, antiphospholipid syndrome) as triggers; guides selection of immunosuppressive regimen
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 3–5 days. Positive ANA and anti-dsDNA directs toward SLE-directed therapy. Antiphospholipid antibodies coexist with acquired hemophilia in some cases, complicating management.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment addresses two simultaneous priorities: controlling acute life-threatening bleeding and eradicating the autoantibody inhibitor. Neither can be ignored — uncontrolled bleeding is immediately fatal while persistent inhibitor will perpetuate the bleeding risk indefinitely.

Hemostatic therapy uses bypassing agents that generate thrombin independently of FVIII. Recombinant activated Factor VIIa (rFVIIa; NovoSeven) 90–120 µg/kg IV every 2–3 hours is one option; activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC; FEIBA) 50–100 IU/kg every 8–12 hours is the other. Both are effective in controlling bleeding in approximately 80–90% of episodes. Since 2021, emicizumab — a bispecific antibody approved for congenital hemophilia A with inhibitors — has been used off-label in acquired hemophilia in several case series with promising results: its subcutaneous dosing (1.5 mg/kg weekly for 4 weeks then 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks) is particularly practical for ongoing prophylaxis during inhibitor eradication. Porcine FVIII (Obizur), licensed specifically for acquired hemophilia, provides an alternative when porcine cross-reactivity of the inhibitor is low.

Inhibitor eradication uses immunosuppression. The ASH 2025 guideline recommends first-line treatment with oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day alone or combined with cyclophosphamide 1.5–2 mg/kg/day in patients with high inhibitor titer, severe bleeding, or an associated condition. Rituximab (anti-CD20, 375 mg/m² weekly for 4 doses) is a second-line option with 60–85% complete remission rates. First-line monotherapy with corticosteroids achieves complete remission in approximately 50–80% within 5 weeks; adding cyclophosphamide increases this to 70–80% and shortens time to remission. Median time to complete remission with immunosuppression is 5 weeks (range 3–40 weeks). IVIG may be used as adjunct in postpartum cases or mild inhibitor.

medicalTreatments.name: Recombinant activated Factor VIIa (rFVIIa; NovoSeven) 90–120 µg/kg IV every 2–3 hours
medicalTreatments.description: First-line bypassing agent for acute bleeding in acquired hemophilia. rFVIIa generates thrombin at the site of vascular injury on tissue factor-expressing cells, bypassing the need for FVIII in the coagulation cascade. Short half-life (2–3 hours) requires frequent dosing; continuous infusion is an alternative.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls bleeding in 86–90% of bleeding episodes (EACH2 registry data; PMID 22212058); equivalent efficacy to aPCC in head-to-head comparison

medicalTreatments.name: Activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC; FEIBA) 50–100 IU/kg every 8–12 hours (maximum 200 IU/kg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Contains activated coagulation factors (FVIIa, FXa) that bypass FVIII in the coagulation cascade. Used as first-line bypassing agent interchangeably with rFVIIa based on availability and patient factors. Contains trace amounts of FVIII, so theoretically can boost inhibitor — rarely clinically relevant.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls bleeding in 86–91% of episodes in published series; thrombotic risk is 1–2% with high doses (above 200 IU/kg/day)

medicalTreatments.name: Emicizumab (Hemlibra) 1.5 mg/kg SC weekly × 4 weeks, then 3 mg/kg SC every 2 weeks (off-label)
medicalTreatments.description: A bispecific antibody bridging FIXa and FX, mimicking the cofactor function of FVIII. Provides hemostatic protection independent of FVIII or bypassing agents. Increasingly used off-label to reduce bleeding burden during the weeks required for immunosuppression to eradicate the inhibitor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the largest case series (Knoebl et al. Blood 2021, PMID 33693659), 90% of patients achieved bleeding control on emicizumab during inhibitor eradication; no thromboembolic events reported

medicalTreatments.name: Porcine FVIII concentrate (Obizur; recombinant porcine FVIII) 200 IU/kg IV initially, then titrate to FVIII level 50–150 IU/dL
medicalTreatments.description: Recombinant porcine FVIII that substitutes for human FVIII. Human anti-FVIII inhibitors often have low cross-reactivity against porcine FVIII, allowing effective hemostatic restoration. Porcine cross-reactivity should be measured before use. Licensed specifically for acquired hemophilia A in the US and Europe.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: FVIII levels normalized in 90% of patients with low porcine cross-reactivity in the phase 2/3 GTH trial; re-bleed rate 28% in the first 24 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day oral, maximum 80 mg) ± cyclophosphamide (1.5–2 mg/kg/day oral)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line immunosuppression for inhibitor eradication. Prednisolone alone achieves complete remission in 50–80% of patients; adding cyclophosphamide accelerates remission. Treatment is continued for at least 3–6 months depending on titer response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete remission in 70–80% with corticosteroids + cyclophosphamide; median time to remission 5 weeks (EACH2 registry, PMID 22212058)

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (375 mg/m² IV weekly × 4 doses) for refractory or relapsed acquired hemophilia
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B cells producing the anti-FVIII autoantibodies. Used as second-line after failed corticosteroids ± cyclophosphamide, or as first-line in frail elderly patients with contraindications to cyclophosphamide.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete remission in 60–85% of patients in retrospective cohort analyses; median time to response 8–12 weeks; longer response duration than cyclophosphamide in some series

surgicalOptions.name: Fasciotomy for compartment syndrome from muscle hematoma
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency decompression of muscle compartments when escalating pressure threatens nerve and vascular integrity. Performed under IV bypassing agent cover to maintain hemostasis. Close hematology and surgical coordination essential.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Limb salvage rates above 90% when fasciotomy is performed within 6 hours of compartment syndrome diagnosis with adequate hemostatic cover

surgicalOptions.name: Image-guided drainage or embolization of large hematomas
surgicalOptions.description: Interventional radiology-guided drainage of retroperitoneal or intramuscular hematomas causing significant mass effect or infection. Requires pre-procedural and post-procedural bypassing agent cover. Embolization of feeding vessels used for pelvic or retroperitoneal hematomas.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success rate 80–90% for arterial embolization of identified bleeding vessels; rebleeding rate 10–20%

alternativeTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG; 1 g/kg/day for 2 days)
alternativeTreatments.description: Saturates Fc receptors and reduces IgG autoantibody half-life; used as adjunct in postpartum acquired hemophilia or in patients with mild inhibitor, particularly when cyclophosphamide is contraindicated. Evidence from case series rather than controlled trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Desmopressin (DDAVP; 0.3 µg/kg IV) in very low-inhibitor titer cases
alternativeTreatments.description: Transiently raises endogenous FVIII by 3–4 fold by releasing FVIII from vascular endothelium. Only useful when FVIII activity is at least partially preserved (> 5 IU/dL) and inhibitor titer is very low. Not recommended as primary therapy for significant bleeding.

whySeeSpecialist: Acquired hemophilia requires immediate specialist hematology evaluation at a hemostasis center because mis-diagnosis leads to dangerous delays — FVIII concentrate infusion in an inhibitor patient is ineffective and wastes time. Bypassing agent dosing, inhibitor eradication protocols, and monitoring of FVIII levels and inhibitor titers require specialist expertise and laboratory support unavailable in most general hospitals.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hematologist at a tertiary hemostasis center or comprehensive hemophilia treatment center (HTC) with experience in inhibitor management. The United Kingdom Haemophilia Centres Doctors Organisation (UKHCDO) and National Hemophilia Foundation networks publish lists of accredited centers. Remote centers should contact their nearest HTC by telephone for same-day guidance when acquired hemophilia is suspected.
hospitalCriteria: Coagulation laboratory capable of APTT mixing studies and Bethesda inhibitor assay
Pharmacy with licensed stock of rFVIIa (NovoSeven), aPCC (FEIBA), and porcine FVIII (Obizur)
Specialist hematologist with hemostasis expertise available within 24 hours
Interventional radiology on-call for hematoma management
ICU capability for critical bleeding with hemodynamic monitoring
keyFacilities: Academic hematology units with hemostasis subspecialty
Interventional radiology units for hematoma embolization
preventionStrategies: Clinicians should have a low threshold for ordering an APTT in any patient presenting with unexpected bruising or bleeding — early detection allows treatment before life-threatening hemorrhage occurs.

When starting immune checkpoint inhibitor cancer therapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab), alert patients to report unexpected bruising or prolonged bleeding; baseline coagulation screen provides a reference if a bleeding complication develops.
Minimize use of penicillin and cephalosporin antibiotics in elderly patients with autoimmune diseases to reduce drug-triggered inhibitor risk when alternatives are clinically equivalent.
In patients with a known previous episode of acquired hemophilia, routine annual APTT monitoring may allow early detection of relapse before major hemorrhage.
Maintain vigilance for acquired hemophilia in postpartum women presenting with unexpected secondary postpartum hemorrhage from day 7 to 6 months after delivery.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid all intramuscular injections, arterial punctures above the compressible level, and invasive procedures without prior consultation with the treating hematologist and coverage with bypassing agents.
Do not take NSAIDs, aspirin, or other antiplatelet agents, which impair platelet function and increase bleeding risk.
Wear a medical alert bracelet identifying acquired hemophilia and the bypassing agent used, in case of emergency.
Report any new swelling, limb pain, or bruising immediately, before pain becomes severe or bleeding becomes extensive.
Maintain follow-up for FVIII levels and inhibitor titers at the frequency specified by the treating hematologist — typically every 1–2 weeks during active immunosuppression and monthly during taper.
Avoid activities with high injury risk (contact sports, heavy lifting) during the period of active inhibitor.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day) during corticosteroid or cyclophosphamide therapy to maintain muscle mass
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks) and vitamin D to offset corticosteroid-induced bone loss
Adequate fluid intake (2–2.5 L/day) during cyclophosphamide therapy to reduce bladder toxicity (hemorrhagic cystitis)
avoid: NSAIDs and aspirin as self-medication for pain from hematomas — they worsen bleeding by inhibiting platelet aggregation
Alcohol during immunosuppressive therapy, as it increases cyclophosphamide hepatotoxicity and corticosteroid-related GI side effects
Grapefruit juice and St John's Wort, which alter metabolism of cyclophosphamide and corticosteroids

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest is required for any active hematoma or bleeding episode until the bypassing agent has stopped hemorrhage and FVIII levels or inhibitor titer improve. Light non-impact activity (gentle walking on level ground) is safe once acute bleeding has resolved. Contact sports, weight training, and activities with fall risk are prohibited until inhibitor is fully eradicated (confirmed by FVIII activity normalization on two consecutive tests) and the treating hematologist has cleared a return to activity.
dailyManagement: Inspect the skin each morning for new or expanding bruising; report any new hematoma, ecchymosis, or swelling to the hematology care team within hours.
Take prescribed corticosteroids (prednisolone) and cyclophosphamide at consistent times each day; do not stop abruptly without medical guidance.
Monitor urine color daily during cyclophosphamide therapy; red or pink urine may indicate hemorrhagic cystitis and requires same-day review.
Monitor blood glucose regularly when on high-dose prednisolone, as steroid-induced hyperglycemia is common in elderly patients.
Keep a bleeding diary recording sites, severity, and duration of any new bleeding events for discussion at follow-up appointments.

Carry the hematology center's emergency contact number at all times and call before seeking emergency care, so specialist bypassing agent guidance can be given over the telephone before the patient arrives.
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the speed of diagnosis, disease severity, and response to immunosuppression. Overall mortality from acquired hemophilia is approximately 8–22% in registry series, with about 70–80% of deaths related to the underlying associated disease (malignancy, sepsis from immunosuppression) rather than uncontrolled bleeding directly. Bleeding-related mortality is approximately 3–10% in modern series where bypassing agents are available promptly. Complete inhibitor remission is achieved in 70–90% of patients with immunosuppression; the median time to remission is approximately 5 weeks with prednisolone plus cyclophosphamide and 8–12 weeks with rituximab. Relapse after remission occurs in 15–20% of patients, usually within the first year. Postpartum acquired hemophilia has the best prognosis: inhibitor remission rates of 70–85% without aggressive immunosuppression in some series, with low mortality. Elderly patients with high inhibitor titers and a serious underlying condition have the worst outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: Acute bleeding is controlled in 80–90% of episodes within 24–48 hours with bypassing agent therapy. Inhibitor eradication typically takes 5–12 weeks with first-line immunosuppression. FVIII levels normalize before or around the time the Bethesda titer becomes undetectable. Immunosuppression is tapered over 3–6 months after complete remission. Annual monitoring for relapse is recommended for 3–5 years.
complications: Life-threatening hemorrhage: retroperitoneal bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage, airway hematoma, and massive GI hemorrhage — the leading cause of acute mortality; requires same-day bypassing agent administration.
Compartment syndrome from muscle hematoma in the forearm, calf, or thigh — limb-threatening if not recognized within 6 hours; fasciotomy under bypassing agent cover may be required.

Infections from immunosuppression: opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis, invasive aspergillus, cytomegalovirus) occur in 10–15% of patients on cyclophosphamide and corticosteroids; prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and antifungal is recommended.
Cyclophosphamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis (5–10% incidence with oral dosing) — prevented by high fluid intake and morning dosing; treated by drug withdrawal and mesna in severe cases.
Corticosteroid complications: hyperglycemia, new-onset hypertension, osteoporosis, adrenal suppression — monitor blood glucose, bone density, and blood pressure during prolonged courses.
Inhibitor relapse in 15–20% of patients after initial remission — managed with a second immunosuppressive course using a different agent or rituximab.

supportResources.name: National Hemophilia Foundation (NHF) — Acquired Hemophilia Resources
supportResources.description: US patient organization providing education, clinical guidance, and patient community resources on acquired hemophilia and inhibitors.

supportResources.name: World Federation of Hemophilia (WFH)
supportResources.description: Global organization supporting people with hemophilia and related disorders; provides guidelines, patient resources, and treatment center directory.

supportResources.name: UK Haemophilia Centre Doctors Organisation (UKHCDO) — Acquired Haemophilia Guidelines
supportResources.description: UKHCDO clinical guidelines and audit data for acquired hemophilia management at UK hemophilia treatment centers.

supportResources.name: Hemophilia Federation of America
supportResources.description: US advocacy and support organization for bleeding disorders including acquired hemophilia; provides insurance guidance and financial assistance resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Antiphospholipid syndrome with lupus anticoagulant
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) also produces a prolonged APTT that fails to correct on mixing, mimicking an inhibitor. However, APS is a prothrombotic, not hemorrhagic, condition; FVIII activity is normal or elevated in APS. Factor assays and lupus anticoagulant confirmatory tests (dRVVT, silica clotting time) distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA vasculitis (allergic purpura)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IgA vasculitis produces palpable purpura from small vessel vasculitis with normal platelet count and normal coagulation studies including APTT. Acquired hemophilia produces deep hematomas, ecchymoses, and a prolonged APTT without skin vasculitis. The clinical pattern and coagulation screen reliably distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TTP produces microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and thrombocytopenia with a normal APTT and normal FVIII. Acquired hemophilia has a severely prolonged APTT with very low FVIII activity and normal or elevated platelet count. Both require urgent specialist management but through entirely different pathways.

confusedWithConditions.name: Aplastic anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aplastic anemia causes pancytopenia (low red cells, white cells, and platelets) with a hypocellular marrow and normal coagulation studies. Acquired hemophilia affects only the coagulation cascade — platelets and marrow are normal, and the bleeding pattern is from hematomas rather than petechiae or mucosal petechial bleeding.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic purpura (IgA vasculitis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are autoimmune-mediated hematological disorders causing abnormal bleeding or purpura requiring specialist hematology evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Aplastic anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are rare, life-threatening conditions causing severe bleeding; aplastic anemia from marrow failure, acquired hemophilia from coagulation factor inhibition.

relatedConditions.name: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Massive hematomas in acquired hemophilia cause acute blood loss anemia, sharing management principles of transfusion support with posthemorrhagic anemia.

relatedConditions.name: Activated protein C resistance (Factor V Leiden)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are disorders of the coagulation system; activated protein C resistance causes thrombosis while acquired hemophilia causes hemorrhage — opposite ends of the coagulation dysregulation spectrum.

faqs.question: What is acquired hemophilia?
faqs.answer: Acquired hemophilia is a rare autoimmune condition where the immune system produces antibodies that attack and neutralize clotting Factor VIII. This causes sudden, severe bleeding in people with no prior bleeding history. Unlike inherited hemophilia, it is not genetic — it develops during a person's lifetime, most often in elderly adults or postpartum women.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of acquired hemophilia?
faqs.answer: The hallmark symptoms are large painful bruises, deep muscle hematomas (especially in the thighs, calves, and forearms), and excessive bleeding from minor cuts or procedures. Joint bleeding is uncommon, unlike inherited hemophilia. Blood in the urine, GI bleeding, and postpartum hemorrhage are also common presentations.

faqs.question: How is acquired hemophilia different from inherited hemophilia?
faqs.answer: Inherited hemophilia is a genetic X-linked disorder affecting males from birth, with joint bleeding as the main feature. Acquired hemophilia occurs in adults of any sex with no family history, causes deep muscle hematomas and mucosal bleeding rather than joint bleeding, and is caused by autoantibodies — not gene mutations. It is treated differently.

faqs.question: What causes acquired hemophilia?
faqs.answer: In about half of cases, no cause is found — these are idiopathic cases mostly in elderly adults. In the other half, causes include autoimmune diseases (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis), pregnancy, cancer, certain drugs (penicillin, immune checkpoint inhibitors), and infections. Identifying and treating the trigger improves outcomes alongside direct inhibitor eradication.

faqs.question: How is acquired hemophilia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis begins with a blood test showing an isolated prolonged APTT (usually above 60 seconds) with a normal prothrombin time and normal platelet count. An APTT mixing study confirms an inhibitor is present rather than a factor deficiency. A Factor VIII activity assay confirms severe FVIII deficiency and a Bethesda assay quantifies the inhibitor titer.

faqs.question: What is a bypassing agent and why is it used?
faqs.answer: A bypassing agent is a drug that helps blood clot without relying on Factor VIII. Because the inhibitor in acquired hemophilia destroys FVIII, giving FVIII concentrate is ineffective. Bypassing agents such as recombinant Factor VIIa (NovoSeven) or activated prothrombin complex concentrate (FEIBA) trigger clotting via alternative pathways, controlling bleeding in 86–90% of episodes.

faqs.question: Can acquired hemophilia be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. With immunosuppression (usually prednisolone with or without cyclophosphamide), the antibody inhibitor is eradicated in 70–90% of patients, allowing Factor VIII to function normally again. Complete remission is achieved in a median of 5 weeks. About 15–20% of patients relapse and require a second treatment course.

faqs.question: Is acquired hemophilia dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated acquired hemophilia carries a mortality of 8–22% in registry data from retroperitoneal and intracranial hemorrhage. However, with prompt use of bypassing agents to control bleeding and immunosuppression to eradicate the inhibitor, most patients achieve remission and full recovery. Early diagnosis is critical.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for acquired hemophilia take?
faqs.answer: Acute bleeding is typically controlled within 24–48 hours of starting bypassing agents. Inhibitor eradication with immunosuppression takes a median of 5 weeks with first-line therapy and up to 12 weeks with rituximab. Immunosuppressants are continued for 3–6 months after remission to reduce relapse risk.

faqs.question: Can women get acquired hemophilia after pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Postpartum acquired hemophilia develops in approximately 8–10% of acquired hemophilia cases, typically 1–4 months after delivery. It presents with unexpected heavy bleeding, large bruises, or failed standard postpartum hemorrhage treatment. Prognosis is generally favorable — inhibitor remission rates are high and mortality is low in postpartum cases.

faqs.question: What is emicizumab and is it used in acquired hemophilia?
faqs.answer: Emicizumab (Hemlibra) is a bispecific antibody that mimics Factor VIII's function by bridging Factor IXa and Factor X. It is approved for congenital hemophilia A with inhibitors and is increasingly used off-label in acquired hemophilia to provide hemostatic protection as a subcutaneous weekly injection while immunosuppression eradicates the inhibitor.

faqs.question: What blood test findings suggest acquired hemophilia?
faqs.answer: Key findings are: isolated prolonged APTT (normal PT and platelets), APTT that does not correct when patient plasma is mixed with normal plasma, very low Factor VIII activity (often below 1 IU/dL), and a positive Bethesda inhibitor assay. This combination in a person without prior bleeding history strongly indicates acquired hemophilia.

faqs.question: Is acquired hemophilia associated with cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 10–15% of acquired hemophilia cases are associated with an underlying malignancy, most commonly solid tumors and lymphomas. All adults over 50 with newly diagnosed acquired hemophilia should have CT imaging to exclude occult cancer. Treating the malignancy alongside inhibitor eradication improves outcomes.

faqs.question: What medications should people with acquired hemophilia avoid?
faqs.answer: NSAIDs, aspirin, and antiplatelet agents should be avoided as they impair platelet function and worsen bleeding. Intramuscular injections must not be given without prior specialist guidance and bypassing agent cover. Arterial puncture in non-compressible sites should be avoided during active inhibitor.

faqs.question: What is the role of rituximab in treating acquired hemophilia?
faqs.answer: Rituximab is a monoclonal antibody that destroys the B cells producing the anti-Factor VIII autoantibodies. It is used as second-line treatment when corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide fail or are contraindicated. It achieves complete remission in 60–85% of patients, with responses typically taking 8–12 weeks to appear.
